result:
When the screen first flickers awake there is always that half-second of expectant black, the hush before the cursor starts breathing again after its swim through liquid crystals. The black rectangle feels, in that hush, like a vacant lot at night where the streetlights are still warming up and every blade of glass has forgotten its color. I like to linger in that limbo because it is not really empty; it is soaked in a density of possibility so complete that any word—bathtub, recidivism, apricot, magma—lands inside it like a single drop of ink in a bath of water, blooming open, diffusing, touching everything yet never filling it. Sometimes I imagine the cursor drumming its chitinous feet together like a wasp circling sugar, impatient for me to begin, but I know the cursor has no agency; only I do, and yet agency itself is a fiction we grant ourselves to keep moving through time. We say we decide, we say we choose, but the deep layers keep shifting regardless, magma under continents.

Years ago, I lived on the southern toe of the Italian boot, in the toe-nail sliver of Calabria that rips into the Ionian Sea. Describing place is always suspect: you have to balance the postcard cliches (“ragged cliffs plunging like jilted brides,” etc.) against the banal odor of diesel buses idling outside the Tabacchi shop. What stays in the mind is almost never the thing you strained to notice. Billboards dissolve, church façades blur, but for some reason I have carried for almost two decades the pattern of damp salt rings deposited on the marble bar top each night by bottom-heavy Peroni bottles. The bartender, a square-headed guy named Milo, never wiped them away until closing time. So when I close my eyes here at the desk back in North America, the whole coastline starts reconstructing itself from those pale frilled circles, as though the world underneath everything is made of effervescent brine and glass.

Memory is the librarian who shelves books by wet fingerprints instead of the Dewey Decimal System; its logic is bodily, not intellectual. I have noticed that whenever a song manages to get salted with deliberate nostalgia—say, some algorithm coughs up an old Eurodance track from 1997—my throat contracts the way it did when I inhaled those summertime diesel fumes outside Catanzaro train station. Neuroscientists would tell me that this is probably an episodic binding error, where viscera mix with tunes on a shared substrate of glutamate and long-term potentiation. They are not wrong, but the vocabulary hollows the experience out the same way a joke dies in a court transcript. What remains valuable is the emotional granularity: the exact curvature of loss plus sweetness, like the gritty bottom of the espresso cup after you have drunk everything except the last bitter decoct of grounds.

Places leak into places: the tiled lobby of my grandfather’s apartment building in Toronto during the ice-bound winter of 1994 still pollinates every marble entryway I walk through. The elevator had mirrors on three sides so that as you rose you encountered infinity versions of yourself carrying groceries in fibrous brown bags whose handles sliced your palms. For weeks after the Northridge earthquake (all the way across the continent in Los Angeles) I stood inside that elevator waiting for its cables to twitch because by then I had learned from television that the planet could shrug an articulate shoulder and flatten your neat stacks of canned beans. I thought: if the elevator fell, how many versions of me would die in each mirrored shard? My ten-year-old subconscious discovered recursion years before I ever saw a line of computer code.

A German poet—I think Rilke—claimed that childhood’s principal terrain is unnamed emotions. Adult life then becomes the futile act of stapling labels onto them. The poet implies development is reductive; maybe he is right. Yet the alternative is unbearable: living at the mercy of unlabeled giants rocking inside us. The first time I saw the Pacific Ocean I was twenty-three and breathless, but the breathlessness was familiar. It was the same curve of throat I employed once while standing on the diving board the summer my father taught me how to swim. The board’s grainy turquoise surface—chlorine-boiled and sun-weathered—had the metallic heat smell I would later encounter again in railway tracks at noon. All of these experiences lie stacked like transparencies in an anatomy textbook; now when I open my mouth at any coastline, the taste of hot aluminum rises up like a memory within memory.

Another version of palimpsest: the first time I watched fireworks from a hospital window. I was fifteen, there overnight for what turned out to be appendicitis, and across the river the city staged its annual celebration with deafening excess. Because the organizers shot the shells simultaneously rather than sequentially, the sky did not so much blossom as convulse—peach, turquoise, white, magenta—in bursts so rapid that my retinas preserved overlapping afterimages: roses blooming into perfect squares. The nurse assigned to me, a woman with wide Guyanese cheekbones and a faint Caribbean tilt in her vowels, leaned against the radiator humming “Yellow Bird,” her eyes fatigued from double shift. I asked if fireworks terrified her; she laughed and said, no, darling, what terrified her was the silence afterward when patients discovered they were alone in the dark with pain. Then the lights in our corridor flickered and a distraught voice somewhere down the hall called for morphine in two languages. When they wheeled me to the operating theater at dawn they let me keep my glasses so I could watch the ceiling tiles kaleidoscope overhead. I decided right there that beauty was not resolution but abundance without end.

Writing is the least efficient way of holding beauty; it is like trying to keep a hurricane inside a newspaper. Still, I keep trying. Perhaps every storyteller is a failed magician who refuses to concede that the rabbit has long since suffocated inside the hat. Moments arrive anyway, small tempests you trap in paragraph cages, hoping a reader will cup the page close enough to feel the spray. The gap between event and language is elastic, stretching until it creaks the way fishing line does before it snaps. One narrates, therefore one edits mercilessly. But every once in a while a detail slips past censorship and grows so incandescent it hurts to look at. Last month I attended a funeral for an uncle whose entire adult life was composed of minor European car repair and minor marital friction; no grand narrative. Yet during the eulogy my cousin recounted the uncle’s habit of collecting single gloves he found on the roadside—limping orphans of snowmelt March—and storing them in a shoebox labeled “Strays.” The room jerked upright at the image. Here was a man aspiring to re-knit the world’s amputations, or maybe indulging the silent fantasy that someday a matched pair would complete itself and send accidental gratitude his way. The glove box will reside forever in whatever strange attic the future prepares for us.

I sometimes wonder whether objects mourn us when we forget them. The childhood teddy bear with the eye dangling by a chewed thread, now mummified in plastic at the back of a closet—does it accumulate layers of intangible dust yearning for the particular grease of my fingerprints? The commonplace of Japanese Shinto—that spirits linger even in chipped rice bowls—doesn't strike me as animist superstition so much as compassionate metaphysics. Extend the logic all the way, and the universe is flooded hourly with bereft artefacts: parking stubs yearning toward the past warmth of glove compartments, abandoned shopping lists grieved by all the unmet cheeses they name. Mourning becomes mundane, cosmic, yet also liberating because it relieves me of needing to hoard souvenirs: somewhere, somehow, the terracotta shard from Pompeii already keeps vigil for me even as it remains lodged in the museum drawer.

Entropy wins, but memory cheats. Aged ninety-two, my grandmother could no longer remember whether milk came from cows or almonds, yet she retained precise stress positions of consonants in the name of the village in Samarkand where her mother was born. She pronounced the word the way one might unfold embroidered silk very slowly—Sy-o-b, with a swallow of air parked between last two letters—after which her face relaxed as if returning by train through decades. On days when the nursing-home staff wheeled patients onto the terrace to absorb vitamin D, Grandma would grip the aluminum rails until veins blued under onion-skin flesh and ask repetitively if “the apples had ripened yet.” Apples? We searched the courtyard: only ornamental crabapples hard as ball bearings. Only later, rummaging through her cedar box, did I find the lithograph of an orchard printed on brittle paper watermarked 1927. In the margin, my great-grandmother had inked a single sentence in Cyrillic whose translation fluttered out like a moth: The apples remember the hands that planted them.

Sometimes a sentence arrives like a bicycle messenger who shouts before he veers away. Years ago, cycling home from a night shift, I coasted past a fenced basketball court lit orange by one sodium lamp. Two teenage boys had climbed the chain-link and were dangling inside, hurling a dented soccer ball at the hoop in slow motion arcs. Each miss clanged the rim with such doleful certainty that the sound echoed under my rib cage. As I rolled past, one kid yelled to the other in Spanish too fast for me to parse, but the cadence lodged itself: three beats, up-down-rest. I have never been able to translate it because the utterance vanished the instant it was born. Still, every so often when I sense my life narrowing into the mailbox slot of efficiency, that orphaned rhythm resurfaces: insistent, bright, as though encouraging me to quit pretending I have an appointment with importance and instead just clamber the fence somewhere.

One morning long after, I overshot my subway stop because I was busy trying to memorize the pattern of rust blooming across the window frame. The train shuddered above a river I hadn’t noticed before. Sunlight ricocheted off water into the carriage and lit floating dust so sharply the particles looked like miniature galaxies. Between stations the driver’s intercom crackled but produced no language; the static itself was eloquent, a surf of lost vowels. I thought suddenly: how many days had I traded for the illusion of getting somewhere on time? At the next station I exited, climbed the stairs, walked four blocks, and bought the greasiest slice of pepperoni available. The kid behind the counter had a black eye turning yellow at the edges and hummed along with a pop song I hated. When I bit into the corner, grease cracked the crust, and the first burst of tomato sauce burned my tongue just enough to prove I was still susceptible.

There is a trick they teach in meditation retreats: picture yourself dead. Not figuratively, but summon the room in five days, its sour air and folded hospital corners; imagine acquaintances conferring about snack platters. Let the image ache so thoroughly it breaks whatever trance of urgency currently possesses you. The exercise is meant to release attachment, yet every time I attempt it I find myself worrying about whether anyone will remember to water the houseplants. My pothos, Amelia, sprawls across the top shelf like an overachieving intern; surely she deserves sustained attention even after my pulse concludes its meticulous ticking. The absurdity comforts me. I cannot fathom cosmic insignificance while simultaneously scheduling irrigation solutions for a single trailing vine.

Last winter a power outage erased an entire city block for two hours. The silence was so unusual that neighbors poured outside half-clothed and speechless. Somewhere a handheld radio spilled jazz, and because the usual traffic hum was missing the trombone solo sounded like a metallic fish leaping. Gradually, people started conferring tin candles scavenged from junk drawers. A child arranged hers in the snow, forming a crooked constellation. Without streetlights, the stars overhead regained jurisdiction. Not romantic; cold. I kept rubbing my sleeves thinking: what capacities have we lost that we never deserved in the first place? Electric glare had erased the Milky Way, yes, but also the intimacy of shared shivering. Under candle-yellow faces looked older, kinder; we might have been villagers centuries ago debating wolves.

I returned inside before power came back on. The apartment was 47 degrees; the thermostat’s digital eyes blank. Wrapped in blankets, I listened: no refrigerator, no Wi-Fi router, no humidifier white-noise. The absence of electricity exposed an under-layer of house sounds—floor joists creaking anesthesia, the neighbor’s aquarium gurgling like distant digestion. Out of nowhere I remembered the sound my mother’s Singer treadle sewing machine made on Sunday afternoons, how the downstairs neighbor once complained that the thump-wheel heartbeat seeped through his ceiling. My mother had laughed, balanced a teacup saucer on her knee, and said, “Tell him it’s the house learning to breathe.” In the blackout the memory arrived complete with olfactory dimensions: lavender talcum, pinking-shears oil, the faint scorch of cloth singed by the iron. Memory as intravenous drip, necromantic and unpolluted.

How does one write despair without glamorizing it? Some mornings I wake saturated by global headlines—warehouse fires, melting permafrost, the perennial applause track of atrocity—until my mind is nothing but an aperture flung open to let horror waltz in. Action feels inadequate, and so does speech. I consider deleting all social media accounts, then remember that disengagement is privilege humming its own lullaby. A friend posts a photograph of a plastic bag snagged on barbed wire; the caption quotes Leonard Cohen: “There is a crack in everything, that’s how the light gets in.” The platitude lands rather like a moth smacking the bulb. Still, the image stays with me because beyond the obvious pollution something else happens: sunlight transfigures the translucent bag into a jellyfish awash midair. Beauty and wound again braided together so tightly that to tug on either thread tightens both.

Mid-October I visited a botanical conservatory with cathedral ceilings where hothouse air pressed like wet wool. Bromeliads kept ironclad secrets in the cups of their leaves; a monarch butterfly—probably released at a wedding overexuberantly—stitched among the orchids. Every step released loamy perfume that hummed with rot as much as bloom. In one corner a cacao tree drooped glossy pods the exact color of a bruise two days post-impact. I touched a pod the way one touches a stranger’s cast: with curiosity held barely above tenderness. A placard explained fermentation—how seeds musty from the pod must sweat together in banana leaves before they concede chocolate. I thought of audience, narrative, everything aching in the dark trying to become sweet.

Because emigration has scattered our family like dandelion seeds, photographs have become amateur wormholes. My aunt’s WhatsApp group blesses us weekly with grainy thumbnail images—uncle at chemo wearing a cap knitted by granddaughter, cousin’s remodeling of kitchen, toddler face-first in birthday cake. Each image arrives pre-smudged by compression algorithms, yet its information is total. My father’s eyebrows now silver crow feathers; my mother’s hands wrist-deep in za’atar dough like decades ago; when I zoom, a single pixel judders with the pistol-cricket we used to catch in jars. Compression losses are vertiginous: a nephew’s missing front tooth summarizes generational time better than any memoir. Meanwhile, the background hums of other chats gossip about political maelstroms we can no longer claim as ours because oceans dilute nationality.

Once during a layover in Singapore Changi airport I fell asleep under the artificial night designed in the butterfly garden—engineered dusk poured every six hours onto transiting bodies. I dreamed of origami giraffes galloping across fold-up boarding passes. When I startled awake, a huge atlas moth clung to my shoulder, wingtips quivering like uncut vinyl. Somewhere a sign declared these insects are bred to live maximum one week. The lifespan felt heroic rather than tragic, perhaps because captive safety allowed their implausible decency: specimens so symmetrical they appeared photoshopped onto being. I wished I could converse: what’s it like to peak on day three? The moth merely fanned again and lifted off, trailing golden scale-spores that settled on my phone screen like infernal glitter. Later, I would swipe calendar reminders and find pigment surviving weeks afterward on the gorilla glass, irradiant as a scold.

There are whole neighborhoods dedicated to loss and replacement: I speak not metaphorically but of literal salvage groceries where dented cans sell for quarters. On Saturdays I wander aisles scanning labels in languages arriving by boat and then mis-docking in discount bins. A tin of smoked octopus glowers at me: “best before February 1922” clearly misprinted. Yet the absurdity feels honest because catastrophe often misprints its date stamp too. Yesterday a jar of pickled okra cost me sixty-nine cents and tasted like summer camp fights over canoe seats. Eating becomes archaeological act; each bite layers removal. I imagine the okra originally grown in soil somewhere reachable only by muddy road that now meets an interstate flyover. Somewhere the farmer has a grandmother who pickles pain into jars as method of tessellating the unbearable distance between past and forward.

Last week lightning split the sycamore behind my building along its longest seam, revealing heartwood pale as biscuit. My upstairs neighbor—a jazz pianist—sat on the back steps that evening biting his lip. Storm had cancelled gig, tree had cancelled symmetry; both of us muttered small condolences. Then from his pocket he extracted a harmonica and coaxed a scale so fragile it felt like whoever invented minor seventh interval had done so specifically for grief-torn wood. After the second chorus the tree itself seemed to respond: a single limb unloosened a flap of bark that slumped groundward with orchestral gravity. Sentient or not, the gesture was enough. We stopped talking; no clearer vocabulary exists than humidity inside our lungs bending notes.

A small girl on the street corner sells ten-minute poems on the spot. I give her four topics—the rain-staved sidewalk cracks, the smell of a bakery’s first batch, the echo inside shut metro turnstiles, and the concept of forgiveness—and she types furiously on a sticker-covered Royal typewriter while I wait. When finished, she hands me a rectangle as perfumed as purple mimeograph ink. The poem describes “forgiveness as bread crust softened by subway-steam in the palms of a commuter who almost missed the night train but didn’t.” I fold the sheet four times and tuck it in my breast pocket over the heart. For the rest of the rainy afternoon my coat whispers in roller-ribbon gusts each time Iplaced train doors exhale.

One more city memory. Construction scaffolding on avenue D shaped a tunnel all summer, manned by crews who jackhammered so insistently the sidewalk deposited powdered granite onto the cuffs of my jeans. Every morning I walked through the nave of din and emerged baptized in pulverized stone that sparkled in sunlight like cheap glamour. The crews spoke Portuguese, and I learned to greet them by Tudo bem? Their smiles broke white against dusk skin and fluorescent vests. End of August we had a minor earthquake, 4.2 on the Richter, barely noticeable unless you were already attuned to subtle cracks. Next day the scaffolding had shifted—love-bite diagonal tilt. They shut the block down, posted yellow tape, posted men in suits who did calculations. By September the entire section was gone, street reopened, pavement black and perfect like never-been-chewed licorice. I miss the dust, the way it stippled my life with evidence of demolition becoming smooth surface yet again.

Books continue multiplying on my shelves even though I reread fewer every year; perhaps they simply want company. The unread ones lean with reproachful spine cracks. One day midweek I decide to read a slim purple volume of Clarice Lispector stories aloud to an empty house, testing whether voice can summon authorial ghost. On page thirty-two Clarice writes: “I have grown tired of autonomous furniture.” My cat, asleep on the arm of the sofa, chooses that instant to slide to the floor with a thump as if to prove non-autonomous servitude. I laugh so hard the neighbor bangs on the wall. Later I discover Clarice died the same year and month as my earliest memory of fluorescent supermarket lights humming over pyramids of oranges that glared like unhatched suns. The coincidence—if it is one—flickers like the last match inside me.

Back in biology class I learned that mitochondria carry separate DNA from the rest of our cells, serving in effect as domesticated bacteria that negotiated tenancy billions of years ago. Thus every heartbeat is a parliament of symbionts harmonizing. I take strange comfort knowing corporal currency is minted by foreign mint; it gives betrayal and loyalty the same porous border. When I am exhausted from cities collapsing into branding algorithms, I place fingertips along pulse until I feel the faint quiver of ancient treaty.

Nighttime right before sleep is the uncanny showroom where day’s leftovers arrange themselves into improbable dioramas. Sometimes the conversations I intended to have instead appear rehearsed under stage lights inside teeth placed too correctly. I command myself to wake tomorrow braver, kinder, to reply to emails, to water aforementioned pothos. The brain, however, has its own itinerary of composting. By dawn only half-remembered hybrids remain—my landlord morphing into my childhood piano teacher, the unpaid parking ticket growing teeth. Neuroscientists label these parasomnias; I call them unpaid residencies where subconscious rehearses for an exhibition titled “Unfinished.” Occasionally the show sells out during daytime too.

Says the graffiti on the bridge parapet: If you can read this, you have already survived every worst day of your past up to now. Spray-painted letters wobble, unsure of their grammar, but their core immunity seems statistically sound. I photograph the line on my phone then deliberately delete it; I want future self to rediscover via unguided encounter.

Lately I have been practicing the principle of anti-projection which decrees that whatever I dislike in others is often what I fear in myself—yet I fail to muster much irritation these days, only a diffuser sadness misting everything. Might this mean fear has deserted me? Hardly. More probable: fear finally found the key under the fake rock and has quietly moved into the spare room.

A voice-mail from mother stores ambient kitchen noise: the kettle whispering its steadfast aria, the fridge magnet rearranging itself minutely under gravity strain. She speaks but I can’t attend to words; these micro-sounds grip me, heritage lullaby paid forward in analog recording. After her death I will still replay this sixty-second file during insomnia to prove rooms once contained inhabitable love.

I confess that writing 2500 words about “whatever” has turned out to be about memory because memory is the only whatever that remains whatever from whatever angle you interrogate. The rest—ambition, appetite, lime-scented candle—becomes hostage to chronology. Meanwhile memory erodes then re-plasters its own walls, shady contractor never out of work. Perhaps the only honest contract between reader and writer is partial disclosure cloaked in generous texture. I try to hand you, not the whole sycamore, but the dust that fell from its wound into my cuff fibers; not great love itself but the closed bakery where croissants once steamed the window opaque when we were both too shy to speak.

Tomorrow is Wednesday, trash day. I will carry out bags containing onion peel, junk mail, dead pens, the flee-skin of identity we daily exfoliate. The sanitation worker swinging the hopper will probably never read this essay; his hands are too busy disciplining gravity. Yet a shard of narrative might adhere to his gloves anyway, the way stardust grazed the vacuum of probes during Apollo missions. Unequal exchange, yes, but who promised reciprocity? The sky has never signed a treaty yet we keep looking up to it for snow, for blue, for the vaguest permission to continue.

Some endings arrive overstitched; others unravel before they reach the seam. When I run out of room, I think of the elderly sign-painter on the side street who gives himself exactly ten brush-strokes per letter before moving on. One day I asked why not linger for perfection. He said, color dries how it dries, and city only pays by the yard. He wiped sap-green across overalls and laughed—a laugh equal parts forgiveness and farewell.

I thought suddenly: how many days had I traded for the illusion of getting somewhere on time? At the next station I exited, climbed the stairs, walked four blocks, and bought the greasiest slice of pepperoni available. The kid behind the counter had a black eye turning yellow at the edges and hummed along with a pop song I hated. When I bit into the corner, grease cracked the crust, and the first burst of tomato sauce burned my tongue just enough to prove I was still susceptible.

There is a trick they teach in meditation retreats: picture yourself dead. Not figuratively, but summon the room in five days, its sour air and folded hospital corners; imagine acquaintances conferring about snack platters. Let the image ache so thoroughly it breaks whatever trance of urgency currently possesses you. The exercise is meant to release attachment, yet every time I attempt it I find myself worrying about whether anyone will remember to water the houseplants. My pothos, Amelia, sprawls across the top shelf like an overachieving intern; surely she deserves sustained attention even after my pulse concludes its meticulous ticking. The absurdity comforts me. I cannot fathom cosmic insignificance while simultaneously scheduling irrigation solutions for a single trailing vine.

Last winter a power outage erased an entire city block for two hours. The silence was so unusual that neighbors poured outside half-clothed and speechless. Somewhere a handheld radio spilled jazz, and because the usual traffic hum was missing the trombone solo sounded like a metallic fish leaping. Gradually, people started conferring tin candles scavenged from junk drawers. A child arranged hers in the snow, forming a crooked constellation. Without streetlights, the stars overhead regained jurisdiction. Not romantic; cold. I kept rubbing my sleeves thinking: what capacities have we lost that we never deserved in the first place? Electric glare had erased the Milky Way, yes, but also the intimacy of shared shivering. Under candle-yellow faces looked older, kinder; we might have been villagers centuries ago debating wolves.

I returned inside before power came back on. The apartment was 47 degrees; the thermostat’s digital eyes blank. Wrapped in blankets, I listened: no refrigerator, no Wi-Fi router, no humidifier white-noise. The absence of electricity exposed an under-layer of house sounds—floor joists creaking anesthesia, the neighbor’s aquarium gurgling like distant digestion. Out of nowhere I remembered the sound my mother’s Singer treadle sewing machine made on Sunday afternoons, how the downstairs neighbor once complained that the thump-wheel heartbeat seeped through his ceiling. My mother had laughed, balanced a teacup saucer on her knee, and said, “Tell him it’s the house learning to breathe.” In the blackout the memory arrived complete with olfactory dimensions: lavender talcum, pinking-shears oil, the faint scorch of cloth singed by the iron. Memory as intravenous drip, necromantic and unpolluted.

How does one write despair without glamorizing it? Some mornings I wake saturated by global headlines—warehouse fires, melting permafrost, the perennial applause track of atrocity—until my mind is nothing but an aperture flung open to let horror waltz in. Action feels inadequate, and so does speech. I consider deleting all social media accounts, then remember that disengagement is privilege humming its own lullaby. A friend posts a photograph of a plastic bag snagged on barbed wire; the caption quotes Leonard Cohen: “There is a crack in everything, that’s how the light gets in.” The platitude lands rather like a moth smacking the bulb. Still, the image stays with me because beyond the obvious pollution something else happens: sunlight transfigures the translucent bag into a jellyfish awash midair. Beauty and wound again braided together so tightly that to tug on either thread tightens both.

Mid-October I visited a botanical conservatory with cathedral ceilings where hothouse air pressed like wet wool. Bromeliads kept ironclad secrets in the cups of their leaves; a monarch butterfly—probably released at a wedding overexuberantly—stitched among the orchids. Every step released loamy perfume that hummed with rot as much as bloom. In one corner a cacao tree drooped glossy pods the exact color of a bruise two days post-impact. I touched a pod the way one touches a stranger’s cast: with curiosity held barely above tenderness. A placard explained fermentation—how seeds musty from the pod must sweat together in banana leaves before they concede chocolate. I thought of audience, narrative, everything aching in the dark trying to become sweet.

Because emigration has scattered our family like dandelion seeds, photographs have become amateur wormholes. My aunt’s WhatsApp group blesses us weekly with grainy thumbnail images—uncle at chemo wearing a cap knitted by granddaughter, cousin’s remodeling of kitchen, toddler face-first in birthday cake. Each image arrives pre-smudged by compression algorithms, yet its information is total. My father’s eyebrows now silver crow feathers; my mother’s hands wrist-deep in za’atar dough like decades ago; when I zoom, a single pixel judders with the pistol-cricket we used to catch in jars. Compression losses are vertiginous: a nephew’s missing front tooth summarizes generational time better than any memoir. Meanwhile, the background hums of other chats gossip about political maelstroms we can no longer claim as ours because oceans dilute nationality.

Once during a layover in Singapore Changi airport I fell asleep under the artificial night designed in the butterfly garden—engineered dusk poured every six hours onto transiting bodies. I dreamed of origami giraffes galloping across fold-up boarding passes. When I startled awake, a huge atlas moth clung to my shoulder, wingtips quivering like uncut vinyl. Somewhere a sign declared these insects are bred to live maximum one week. The lifespan felt heroic rather than tragic, perhaps because captive safety allowed their implausible decency: specimens so symmetrical they appeared photoshopped onto being. I wished I could converse: what’s it like to peak on day three? The moth merely fanned again and lifted off, trailing golden scale-spores that settled on my phone screen like infernal glitter. Later, I would swipe calendar reminders and find pigment surviving weeks afterward on the gorilla glass, irradiant as a scold.

There are whole neighborhoods dedicated to loss and replacement: I speak not metaphorically but of literal salvage groceries where dented cans sell for quarters. On Saturdays I wander aisles scanning labels in languages arriving by boat and then mis-docking in discount bins. A tin of smoked octopus glowers at me: “best before February 1922” clearly misprinted. Yet the absurdity feels honest because catastrophe often misprints its date stamp too. Yesterday a jar of pickled okra cost me sixty-nine cents and tasted like summer camp fights over canoe seats. Eating becomes archaeological act; each bite layers removal. I imagine the okra originally grown in soil somewhere reachable only by muddy road that now meets an interstate flyover. Somewhere the farmer has a grandmother who pickles pain into jars as method of tessellating the unbearable distance between past and forward.

Last week lightning split the sycamore behind my building along its longest seam, revealing heartwood pale as biscuit. My upstairs neighbor—a jazz pianist—sat on the back steps that evening biting his lip. Storm had cancelled gig, tree had cancelled symmetry; both of us muttered small condolences. Then from his pocket he extracted a harmonica and coaxed a scale so fragile it felt like whoever invented minor seventh interval had done so specifically for grief-torn wood. After the second chorus the tree itself seemed to respond: a single limb unloosened a flap of bark that slumped groundward with orchestral gravity. Sentient or not, the gesture was enough. We stopped talking; no clearer vocabulary exists than humidity inside our lungs bending notes.

A small girl on the street corner sells ten-minute poems on the spot. I give her four topics—the rain-staved sidewalk cracks, the smell of a bakery’s first batch, the echo inside shut metro turnstiles, and the concept of forgiveness—and she types furiously on a sticker-covered Royal typewriter while I wait. When finished, she hands me a rectangle as perfumed as purple mimeograph ink. The poem describes “forgiveness as bread crust softened by subway-steam in the palms of a commuter who almost missed the night train but didn’t.” I fold the sheet four times and tuck it in my breast pocket over the heart. For the rest of the rainy afternoon my coat whispers in roller-ribbon gusts each time Iplaced train doors exhale.

One more city memory. Construction scaffolding on avenue D shaped a tunnel all summer, manned by crews who jackhammered so insistently the sidewalk deposited powdered granite onto the cuffs of my jeans. Every morning I walked through the nave of din and emerged baptized in pulverized stone that sparkled in sunlight like cheap glamour. The crews spoke Portuguese, and I learned to greet them by Tudo bem? Their smiles broke white against dusk skin and fluorescent vests. End of August we had a minor earthquake, 4.2 on the Richter, barely noticeable unless you were already attuned to subtle cracks. Next day the scaffolding had shifted—love-bite diagonal tilt. They shut the block down, posted yellow tape, posted men in suits who did calculations. By September the entire section was gone, street reopened, pavement black and perfect like never-been-chewed licorice. I miss the dust, the way it stippled my life with evidence of demolition becoming smooth surface yet again.

Books continue multiplying on my shelves even though I reread fewer every year; perhaps they simply want company. The unread ones lean with reproachful spine cracks. One day midweek I decide to read a slim purple volume of Clarice Lispector stories aloud to an empty house, testing whether voice can summon authorial ghost. On page thirty-two Clarice writes: “I have grown tired of autonomous furniture.” My cat, asleep on the arm of the sofa, chooses that instant to slide to the floor with a thump as if to prove non-autonomous servitude. I laugh so hard the neighbor bangs on the wall. Later I discover Clarice died the same year and month as my earliest memory of fluorescent supermarket lights humming over pyramids of oranges that glared like unhatched suns. The coincidence—if it is one—flickers like the last match inside me.

Back in biology class I learned that mitochondria carry separate DNA from the rest of our cells, serving in effect as domesticated bacteria that negotiated tenancy billions of years ago. Thus every heartbeat is a parliament of symbionts harmonizing. I take strange comfort knowing corporal currency is minted by foreign mint; it gives betrayal and loyalty the same porous border. When I am exhausted from cities collapsing into branding algorithms, I place fingertips along pulse until I feel the faint quiver of ancient treaty.

Nighttime right before sleep is the uncanny showroom where day’s leftovers arrange themselves into improbable dioramas. Sometimes the conversations I intended to have instead appear rehearsed under stage lights inside teeth placed too correctly. I command myself to wake tomorrow braver, kinder, to reply to emails, to water aforementioned pothos. The brain, however, has its own itinerary of composting. By dawn only half-remembered hybrids remain—my landlord morphing into my childhood piano teacher, the unpaid parking ticket growing teeth. Neuroscientists label these parasomnias; I call them unpaid residencies where subconscious rehearses for an exhibition titled “Unfinished.” Occasionally the show sells out during daytime too.

Says the graffiti on the bridge parapet: If you can read this, you have already survived every worst day of your past up to now. Spray-painted letters wobble, unsure of their grammar, but their core immunity seems statistically sound. I photograph the line on my phone then deliberately delete it; I want future self to rediscover via unguided encounter.

Lately I have been practicing the principle of anti-projection which decrees that whatever I dislike in others is often what I fear in myself—yet I fail to muster much irritation these days, only a diffuser sadness misting everything. Might this mean fear has deserted me? Hardly. More probable: fear finally found the key under the fake rock and has quietly moved into the spare room.

A voice-mail from mother stores ambient kitchen noise: the kettle whispering its steadfast aria, the fridge magnet rearranging itself minutely under gravity strain. She speaks but I can’t attend to words; these micro-sounds grip me, heritage lullaby paid forward in analog recording. After her death I will still replay this sixty-second file during insomnia to prove rooms once contained inhabitable love.

I confess that writing 2500 words about “whatever” has turned out to be about memory because memory is the only whatever that remains whatever from whatever angle you interrogate. The rest—ambition, appetite, lime-scented candle—becomes hostage to chronology. Meanwhile memory erodes then re-plasters its own walls, shady contractor never out of work. Perhaps the only honest contract between reader and writer is partial disclosure cloaked in generous texture. I try to hand you, not the whole sycamore, but the dust that fell from its wound into my cuff fibers; not great love itself but the closed bakery where croissants once steamed the window opaque when we were both too shy to speak.

Tomorrow is Wednesday, trash day. I will carry out bags containing onion peel, junk mail, dead pens, the flee-skin of identity we daily exfoliate. The sanitation worker swinging the hopper will probably never read this essay; his hands are too busy disciplining gravity. Yet a shard of narrative might adhere to his gloves anyway, the way stardust grazed the vacuum of probes during Apollo missions. Unequal exchange, yes, but who promised reciprocity? The sky has never signed a treaty yet we keep looking up to it for snow, for blue, for the vaguest permission to continue.

Some endings arrive overstitched; others unravel before they reach the seam. When I run out of room, I think of the elderly sign-painter on the side street who gives himself exactly ten brush-strokes per letter before moving on. One day I asked why not linger for perfection. He said, color dries how it dries, and city only pays by the yard. He wiped sap-green across overalls and laughed—a laugh equal parts forgiveness and farewell.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
